title: Characterizing the functional role of quantum coherence in energy transfer
abstract: Quantum coherence is understood to play a role in excitation energy transfer in open quantum systems, yet a quantitative approach to assessing its influence on the transfer process is still missing. Using Nakajima-Zwanzig projection operators, we derive a general memory kernel identity that enables us to characterize and quantify the impact of coherence in the eigenenergy basis on a generalized rate of energy transfer. Applying our approach to the electronic dynamics of a dimer coupled to a structured phonon bath, we demonstrate how quantum coherence acts to modulate energy transfer.
summary: This paper develops a general theoretical framework using projection operators and memory kernels to quantify how quantum coherence influences energy transfer rates. By applying this to an electronic dimer model coupled to a structured bath, the authors demonstrate that coherence can act as a switch, either boosting or dampening the rate depending on system parameters.
main_problem: The primary goal is to quantitatively characterize the functional role of quantum coherence in modulating energy transfer rates within open quantum systems.
main_result: Quantum coherence can either enhance or suppress the overall rate of energy transfer, depending on system parameters like detuning and coupling strength. A figure of merit quantifies this relative contribution.
method: The authors employ Nakajima-Zwanzig projection operators to derive a generalized memory kernel identity, allowing for the separation of coherent and incoherent contributions to the dynamics.
model_or_system: The study uses an electronic dimer model (two coupled sites) interacting with structured phonon baths. The dynamics are governed by the Liouville-von Neumann equation, analyzed using techniques derived from HEOM.
key_observables: The figure of merit $f_{\alpha	o eta} = 1 - k^{(R)}_{\alpha	o eta}/k^{(Q)}_{\alpha	o eta}$ quantifies coherence's relative impact on the energy transfer rate.
important_parameters: Electronic detuning ($\Delta\epsilon$), coupling strength ($V$), reorganization energy ($\lambda$), and spectral density parameters ($\gamma, \Omega$).
main_assumptions: The analysis often assumes a stationary state limit ($t 	o \infty$) and utilizes the HEOM framework, which requires truncating an infinite hierarchy.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate $f_{2	o 1}$ as a function of detuning ($\Delta\epsilon$) or reorganization energy ($\lambda$), showing distinct regimes of coherence-induced suppression (gray) or enhancement (pink).
paper_structure: The paper develops the theoretical framework using projection operators to derive memory kernel identities, applies this formalism to the dimer model dynamics via HEOM, and analyzes how specific parameters tune the resulting rate modification quantified by $f$.
why_interesting: This work provides a rigorous, quantitative tool ($f$) to diagnose coherence effects in energy transfer, which is crucial for designing artificial light-harvesting systems or understanding biological processes where coherent dynamics are suspected.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Energy transfer in light-harvesting antennae is mod- eled as an open quantum system interacting with a local vi- brational environment. As an example, we show here two electronically coupled bacteriochlorophylls forming a dimer. The concurrent influence of the environment and quantum coherence to state transfer is described by the total memory kernel KQ(τ). We introduce the decomposition KQ(τ)P = KR(τ)P + KR(τ)C with KR(τ) being the environment mem- ory kernel, which allows us to directly quantify the extent to which the rate of energy transfer depends on quantum coher- ence via the term KR(τ)C.

FIG. 2. The figure of merit f2→1 as a function of detuning in a dimer coupled to an overdamped Brownian oscillator. The gray and pink regions correspond to regimes where the func- tional role of quantum coherence is to suppress and enhance the energy transfer process, respectively. The inset shows the corresponding total (solid line) and thermal (dashed line) rates of the downhill energy transfer process. The two rates deviate for intermediate detuning, demonstrating the impact of quantum coherence on the transfer process.

FIG. 3. The figure of merit f2→1 as a function of the re- organization energy of the overdamped Brownian oscillator, with ∆ϵ = 400 cm−1. The gray and pink regions correspond to regimes where the functional role of quantum coherence is to suppress and enhance the energy transfer process, respec- tively. The inset shows the corresponding total (solid line) and thermal (dashed line) rates of the downhill transfer pro- cess. The star marker indicates the point where the optimal value of k(Q) 2→1 is achieved, which in this example occurs when f2→1 < 0.
